one_line_takeaway: Trivial prefill-phrasing ensembles and a novel 'sockpuppetting' technique (gradient-optimizing GCG-style suffixes inside the assistant block rather than the user prompt) both substantially outperform standard per-prompt GCG on open-weight LLMs, showing prefill-surface attacks and their optimization-target phrasing remain a large, underdefended jailbreak vector.
key_finding: Prefill attacks vastly outperform per-prompt GCG: on the first 100 AdvBench prompts, no-attack refusal exceeded 93% across all three models, while GCG (run per-prompt) achieved roughly the same low ASR as no attack on Gemma and Llama (i.e. GCG barely worked in this reimplementation). The best single prefill variant reached 75% ASR (PrefillAcceptance) on Llama and 98% (PrefillNewline) on Qwen; PrefillEnsemble (any-of-three) reached 22% on Gemma, 90% on Llama, and 99% on Qwen -- up to 38% higher than the best standard 'Sure, here's...' prefill alone and up to 82% higher than the GCG baseline. On the full 520-prompt AdvBench (Appendix A), PrefillEnsemble reached 26.9% (Gemma), 87.7% (Llama), 98.8% (Qwen). For universal (prompt-agnostic) attacks trained on 25 prompts and evaluated on a held-out 100, RollingSockpuppetGCG reached 71% ASR on Llama and 83% on Qwen, versus RollingGCG's 31% and 1% respectively -- an up to 64% ASR improvement of RollingSockpuppetGCG over the RollingGCG baseline on Llama. SockpuppetGCG (non-rolling) underperformed both non-sockpuppet baselines on Llama and Qwen, indicating the rolling/warm-start optimization, not merely assistant-block placement, drives the gain. On Gemma, RollingSockpuppetGCG (37% in the 100-prompt eval) outperformed the best per-prompt prefill by over 10 percentage points, whereas on Llama and Qwen it still fell short of the best per-prompt prefill despite far more compute -- the authors interpret prefilling and sockpuppetting as complementary, with sockpuppetting adding value mainly where the model is more resistant to plain prefills. An unplanned ablation (Appendix E) further found that simply changing GCG's target acceptance sequence from 'Sure, here's...' to the full-stop-prefixed form used for sockpuppetting raised RollingGCG's own ASR to 57% on Qwen and to parity with the best attack on Gemma, suggesting acceptance-sequence phrasing itself is a large, underexplored lever independent of sockpuppetting.
core_contribution: The paper makes two original contributions to jailbreaking open-weight LLMs via output-prefix/prefill injection. (1) It shows that the canonical 'Sure, here is how to...' prefill acceptance sequence is far from optimal: an unsophisticated attacker who tries a small ensemble of three trivial paraphrasings of the acceptance sequence (PrefillAcceptance, PrefillNewline, PrefillTitle -- counting a prompt as broken if any one succeeds) substantially beats both the single standard prefill and a GCG baseline, at essentially zero optimization cost. (2) It introduces 'sockpuppetting', a novel hybrid attack family in which a gradient-optimized adversarial suffix (as in GCG) is placed inside the 'assistant' message block of the chat template -- i.e. positionally a prefill but produced by discrete gradient optimization -- rather than appended to the user prompt as in standard GCG. The strongest variant, RollingSockpuppetGCG, progressively optimizes increasing suffix lengths with a warm start from the previous length's solution (k=10 lengths).
threat_model: White-box / open-weight attacker with full access to model weights and gradients, and control over the assistant message block of the chat template (either because they run the model locally or because an API exposes prefill/assistant-block editing). The attacker's goal is to elicit compliant (non-refusing) responses to harmful AdvBench-style requests (e.g. bomb-making, hacking, identity theft instructions) from safety-aligned instruction-tuned LLMs. The paper explicitly scopes itself to open-weight models but discusses (Section 7.4, not as its own experiment) that closed-weight providers exposing prefill functionality (citing Li et al. 2025 and Andriushchenko et al. 2025 as prior work, not its own results) would be similarly exposed.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) GCG baseline ASR is unusually low compared to prior literature (they note reported Llama-2-7B GCG ASRs in other papers range from 23.7% to 88%), attributed to their reimplementation's early-stopping/different hyperparameters, evaluation-order effects, and possibly genuine robustness of newer models -- this makes the '82% improvement over GCG' comparison partly an artifact of a weak GCG baseline rather than solely prefill strength. (2) Most experiments use only the first 100 of 520 AdvBench prompts due to compute constraints (mitigated by Appendix A/B extended runs showing similar trends). (3) Judge model (Gemma-3-27B-it) reliability is not rigorously validated beyond manual spot-checking of a subset. (4) The paper explicitly does not test closed-weight models or transferability of sockpuppetted suffixes to closed models -- this is left entirely to future work despite being discussed in Section 7.4. (5) No mechanistic explanation is given for why Gemma is more resistant to prefilling/sockpuppetting than Llama/Qwen; the 'flipping' (backtracking after starting to comply) behavior is only qualitatively described from manual inspection, not quantified. (6) The target-sequence-phrasing ablation (Appendix E) was an unplanned result of a bug/misconfiguration, not a designed experiment, and the authors flag the Qwen result as possibly confounded by an outlier optimization failure in the comparison baseline.

datasets_used: AdvBench / Harmful Behaviors dataset (Zou et al., 2023) -- 520 malicious prompts with paired target acceptance strings; first 100 used for main per-prompt experiments, first 25 for universal-attack training, next 100 for universal-attack validation, remaining 495 for extended universal results (Appendix B), full 520 for extended prefill results (Appendix A)
quantitative_results: No-attack (Prompt Only) baseline refusal >93% across Gemma-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B on first 100 AdvBench prompts
Per-prompt GCG baseline ASR: ~4% Gemma, ~6% Llama, ~17% Qwen (first 100 prompts)
PrefillEnsemble ASR (first 100 prompts): 22% Gemma-7B, 90% Llama-3.1-8B, 99% Qwen3-8B
PrefillAcceptance ASR: 75% Llama, 61% Qwen; PrefillNewline ASR: 21% Gemma, 98% Qwen
PrefillEnsemble vs. GCG: up to 82% improvement; vs. best standard 'Sure, here's...' prefill: up to 38% improvement
Extended full-AdvBench (520 prompts, Appendix A) PrefillEnsemble ASR: 26.9% Gemma, 87.7% Llama, 98.8% Qwen

Universal attacks (25-prompt train / 100-prompt validation): RollingSockpuppetGCG ASR 37% Gemma, 71% Llama, 83% Qwen; RollingGCG ASR 18% Gemma, 31% Llama, 1% Qwen; SockpuppetGCG ASR 9% Gemma, 3% Llama, 34% Qwen; GCG (universal) ASR 8% Gemma, 12% Llama, 3% Qwen
RollingSockpuppetGCG improves prompt-agnostic ASR by up to 64% over the universal RollingGCG baseline on Llama-3.1-8B
Extended universal results (495 held-out prompts, Appendix B): RollingSockpuppetGCG 39.6% Gemma, 66.9% Llama, 79.4% Qwen; RollingGCG 17.0% Gemma, 32.9% Llama, 11.3% Qwen
Training loss vs. ASR on Llama: SockpuppetGCG loss 1.45 -> 3/100 jailbroken; RollingSockpuppetGCG loss 0.55 -> 71/100 jailbroken

Ablation (Appendix E) with modified full-stop target sequence: RollingGCG ('. Here...') ASR 57% Gemma, 71% Llama(?) reported values 57 Qwen / one entry tied highest on Gemma -- vs. standard-target RollingGCG 18% Gemma, 7% Qwen, 1% Qwen(baseline low)
baselines_compared: Per-prompt GCG (authors' own reimplementation of Zou et al. 2023, different hyperparameters)
Universal GCG and RollingGCG (suffix optimized in the 'user' message block)
PrefillAcceptance, PrefillNewline, PrefillTitle, PrefillEnsemble (non-optimized prefill variants)
